hyperlipidemia: high levels of fat (like cholesterol) in the blood
infarction: tissue death caused by a blocked blood supply
ischemia: reduced blood flow to a part of the body
thrombosis: a blood clot blocking a blood vessel
embolism: a blockage in a blood vessel, often by a clot
aneurysm: a bulge in a weakened blood vessel wall
atherosclerosis: hardening and narrowing of the arteries
hematoma: a pool of blood collected outside a blood vessel (a bad bruise)
edema: swelling caused by extra fluid in the body
sepsis: a dangerous body-wide reaction to an infection
pneumonia: an infection that inflames the air sacs in the lungs
bronchitis: inflammation of the airways in the lungs
dyspnea: shortness of breath or difficulty breathing
hypoxia: not enough oxygen reaching the body's tissues
anemia: having too few healthy red blood cells
leukemia: a cancer of the blood and bone marrow
lymphoma: a cancer of the immune system's lymph cells
carcinoma: a common type of cancer that starts in the skin or tissues
metastasis: the spread of cancer to other parts of the body
benign: not cancerous and not able to spread
biopsy: removing a small sample of tissue to test it
neoplasm: an abnormal growth of tissue (a tumor)
prognosis: the likely course or outcome of an illness
etiology: the cause or origin of a disease
inflammation: redness, swelling, and heat as the body responds to injury
arthritis: painful swelling and stiffness of the joints
osteoporosis: weak, brittle bones that break easily
laceration: a deep cut or tear in the skin
glucose: sugar in the blood that the body uses for energy
insulin: a hormone that controls blood sugar
diabetes: a condition where blood sugar is too high
dialysis: a treatment that filters the blood when kidneys fail
endoscopy: looking inside the body with a thin camera tube
colonoscopy: an exam of the large intestine using a camera
neuropathy: nerve damage causing pain, numbness, or weakness
seizure: a sudden burst of abnormal electrical activity in the brain
meningitis: inflammation of the lining around the brain and spinal cord
antibiotic: a medicine that fights bacterial infections
analgesic: a medicine that relieves pain (a painkiller)
anesthesia: medicine that blocks pain, often during surgery
anticoagulant: a medicine that helps prevent blood clots (a blood thinner)
contraindication: a reason a treatment should not be used
intramuscular: given by injection into a muscle
subcutaneous: given by injection just under the skin
immunization: protection against a disease, usually by a vaccine
antibody: a protein the immune system makes to fight germs
antigen: a substance that triggers an immune response
lesion: an area of damaged or abnormal tissue
abscess: a painful, pus-filled pocket caused by infection
melanoma: a serious type of skin cancer
catheter: a thin tube put into the body to deliver or drain fluids
suture: a stitch used to close a wound
ambulatory: able to walk; not needing to stay in bed
prophylaxis: treatment given to prevent a disease
palliative: care focused on comfort rather than cure
remission: a period when disease symptoms lessen or disappear
